Experiment_description: This stage implements and evaluates baseline Failure-Aware Code Generation (FACG) systems across multiple experimental configurations. All experiments compare standard code generation prompting against FACG prompting that incorporates contrastive examples from a failure pattern library. Three distinct setups were tested: (A) a programming problems dataset showing modest 2.6% improvement, (B) an imbalanced failure library setup showing 45% improvement with high volatility, and (C) a synthetic 6-problem dataset with balanced failure patterns showing consistent 33.7% improvement. All experiments use simulated/synthetic code generation with rule-based approaches and execution-based evaluation.
Significance: These experiments provide initial evidence that contrastive learning from execution failures can improve code generation quality, with effect sizes ranging from 2.6% to 45% depending on experimental configuration. The consistent finding of variance reduction (in addition to mean improvement) suggests FACG prevents catastrophic semantic failures rather than only occasionally fixing outputs. The sensitivity of results to failure library composition and retrieval quality highlights critical design factors for future work. However, all experiments use synthetic/simulated setups, so generalization to real code generation models and benchmarks remains to be validated.
Description: Each experiment follows a similar workflow: (1) create or use a set of programming problems with test cases, (2) generate initial code solutions using either standard prompting (baseline) or FACG prompting with retrieved failure patterns, (3) execute generated code and collect pass@1 rates, (4) build failure pattern libraries by clustering execution failures, and (5) compare baseline vs FACG performance. Setup C (Nodes 7c453b8dbc024d219c7cb3f1a547f25c, b393e3afa87143c7afa926a8b12524cc, e2b789cce08d4c478c672ca258d65131) uses 6 synthetic problems (find_max, sum_list, count_occurrences, contains_substring, filter_even, reverse_string) evaluated over 50 trials. The failure library contains logic_error (3), type_error (2), boundary_error (2), and single instances of off_by_one, index_error, and null_handling. Setup B (Node ace12afe0a2d4ae7b3818f3f9e936334) uses 20 evaluation rounds with an imbalanced library dominated by off_by_one errors (16 instances). Setup A (Node 21206e9429f14a33bb6d792ec5bc3cbf) uses 10 trials on programming problems but shows inconsistent failure counting.

List_of_included_plots.description: Pass@1 bar chart on a synthetic code generation setup shows FACG outperforming the baseline: baseline mean 0.673 with std 0.179 vs FACG mean 0.900 with std 0.115. The annotated relative improvement is 33.7% (0.900/0.673−1). The error bars overlap slightly (baseline upper ~0.852; FACG lower ~0.785), but the mean gap is large (Δ=0.227 absolute), suggesting a robust improvement even with variance.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot demonstrates the primary finding from the most consistent experimental setup (Setup C): FACG achieves a large absolute improvement (+0.227 pass@1, 33.7% relative) with reduced variance (0.115 vs 0.179 std). The reduced variance is particularly important as it suggests FACG prevents catastrophic failures rather than only occasionally helping. The overlapping error bars indicate the need for statistical significance testing, but the large mean separation relative to FACG's tight variance suggests a robust effect.

List_of_included_plots.description: Per-problem success rates (six synthetic problems) show consistent gains for FACG across all tasks: contains_substring ~0.58→~0.86, filter_even ~0.62→~0.88, reverse_string ~0.54→~0.86, find_max ~0.76→~0.96, sum_list ~0.80→~0.94, count_occurrences ~0.74→~0.90.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This per-problem breakdown is scientifically valuable because it shows FACG improves uniformly across all tasks rather than being driven by outliers. Critically, the largest gains occur on the hardest baseline tasks (reverse_string, contains_substring: +0.28 to +0.32), suggesting FACG specifically helps with edge cases and semantic pitfalls. The smallest gains on already-strong tasks (sum_list, find_max) show diminishing returns when baseline is high, consistent with FACG preventing common errors rather than enabling fundamentally new capabilities.

List_of_included_plots.description: Failure pattern distribution (counts) in the synthetic dataset: logic_error is most frequent (3), followed by type_error (2) and boundary_error (2); off_by_one (1), index_error (1), and null_handling (1) are less frequent.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot reveals the failure library composition in Setup C, which is relatively balanced across semantic error categories (logic, type, boundary). The small total counts (10 patterns) indicate limited coverage, yet FACG still achieves substantial improvements, suggesting even a modest library of interpretable failure modes provides useful negative guidance. Comparing this to Setup B's imbalanced library (dominated by off_by_one) helps explain why Setup C shows more consistent improvements: better coverage of diverse error types enables more reliable retrieval.

List_of_included_plots.description: Pass@1 across trials (single large plot) reinforces the stability story: FACG stays above the baseline mean line for most trials and often hits 1.0, whereas baseline oscillates substantially and includes a pronounced collapse near trial ~32 (~0.17).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This trial-wise view provides evidence that FACG reduces tail-risk: baseline has multiple severe failures (down to 0.17) while FACG maintains higher minimum performance. The near-constant FACG performance after initial trials suggests stable behavior once the failure library is established. However, FACG still has occasional dips (to ~0.5), indicating some failure modes remain unaddressed—likely those not represented in the small failure library or cases where retrieval fails.

List_of_included_plots.description: Pass@1 across 20 evaluation rounds shows FACG prompting (orange) consistently above standard prompting (blue), with FACG frequently hitting 1.0 while standard has several low rounds (including near 0 around rounds 2–3). The dashed mean lines indicate ~0.55 for standard vs ~0.80 for FACG, an absolute gain of ~0.25 (≈45% relative).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot from Setup B shows the largest relative improvement (45%) but also reveals high volatility in both methods. The frequent FACG peaks at 1.0 contrasted with baseline's catastrophic failures (near 0) suggest FACG can dramatically reduce worst-case performance. However, the high variance and occasional FACG underperformance in early rounds indicate sensitivity to retrieval quality and initialization. This setup's imbalanced failure library (dominated by off_by_one) may explain why improvements are large but inconsistent.

List_of_included_plots.description: Failure-pattern library distribution is imbalanced: off_by_one dominates (count ~16), while empty_list and wrong_range are moderate (~8 each) and wrong_init is lowest (~5).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This imbalanced library composition in Setup B contrasts sharply with Setup C's more balanced distribution. The dominance of off_by_one errors (16/37 patterns) likely biases retrieval toward boundary/indexing issues, which may explain both the large improvements on affected tasks and the high variance (poor performance when other error types occur). This comparison across setups provides important insight: failure library balance and coverage are critical design factors for FACG systems.

Key_numerical_results.description: FACG mean pass@1 in Setup C (synthetic 6-problem dataset, 50 trials)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: This represents the best-case performance from the most consistent experimental setup. The 0.900 pass@1 with reduced variance (0.115 std) demonstrates that contrastive failure-pattern guidance can achieve high accuracy while maintaining stability across trials.

Key_numerical_results.description: Baseline mean pass@1 in Setup C
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Baseline performance of 0.673 with higher variance (0.179 std) serves as the comparison point. The gap to FACG (0.227 absolute difference) represents a 33.7% relative improvement, which is substantial for a preliminary implementation.

Key_numerical_results.description: Absolute pass@1 improvement (FACG - baseline) in Setup C
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The +0.227 absolute improvement is large and consistent across the 50 trials, suggesting a robust effect. This is the most reliable improvement estimate from the stage, as Setup C shows the most consistent results across multiple identical runs (three nodes with identical outcomes).

Key_numerical_results.description: Relative improvement percentage in Setup C
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The 33.7% relative improvement demonstrates substantial gains from incorporating failure patterns as contrastive examples. This is more conservative than Setup B's 45% but more reliable given the lower variance and consistent replication.

Key_numerical_results.description: FACG standard deviation in Setup C
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The reduced standard deviation (0.115 vs baseline 0.179) is scientifically important beyond the mean improvement. It indicates FACG not only improves average performance but also stabilizes generation by preventing catastrophic semantic failures, which is critical for practical deployment.

Key_numerical_results.description: FACG mean pass@1 in Setup B (imbalanced library, 20 rounds)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Setup B achieves 0.80 pass@1 (vs baseline 0.55), a 45% relative improvement, but with much higher variance than Setup C. This suggests that when the failure library happens to match the task distribution well (e.g., many boundary errors), FACG can achieve very large gains, but the approach is sensitive to library composition.

Key_numerical_results.description: Absolute pass@1 improvement in Setup A (0.6500 - 0.6333)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Setup A shows only a modest 2.6% improvement, dramatically lower than Setups B and C. This large variation (2.6% to 45% across setups) reveals that FACG effectiveness is highly sensitive to experimental configuration, including failure library quality, retrieval mechanism, and evaluation protocol. This sensitivity is a critical finding for future work.

Key_numerical_results.description: Largest per-problem improvement in Setup C (reverse_string task)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The reverse_string task shows the largest gain (+0.32, from 0.54 to 0.86), indicating FACG is most effective on tasks prone to edge-case and boundary errors. This aligns with the failure library's composition (boundary_error, off_by_one patterns) and supports the hypothesis that contrastive failure examples help prevent specific semantic error categories.
